narrative_blocks.header: Two wives lost, a third marriage, then gone
narrative_blocks.body: Seth married three times in fifteen years. Anna died in 1799, the widow Lydia he married next died in 1812, and after a third marriage that same year he was himself dead within eighteen months.

narrative_blocks.header: Two wives lost, a third marriage, then gone
narrative_blocks.body: Seth married three times in fifteen years. Anna died in 1799, the widow Lydia he married next died in 1812, and after a third marriage that same year he was himself dead within eighteen months.

narrative_blocks.header: She died days after her last baby's baptism
narrative_blocks.body: Anna bore Seth a houseful of children through the 1780s and 1790s. She died in June 1799, eleven days after the baptism of her last infant.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister both married Buckleys
narrative_blocks.body: Nancy married Chauncey Buckley at Rocky Hill in 1805. Her sister Abigail had married John Buckley in the same town the year before.

narrative_blocks.header: Norman died young
narrative_blocks.body: Norman died young in 1794.

narrative_blocks.header: Seth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Seth died young in 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Infant died young
narrative_blocks.body: Infant died young in 1799.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the family west to Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Hart left Connecticut for Massachusetts and New York and finally settled in Bronson, Michigan, where he died in 1852.

narrative_blocks.header: He married two Carr sisters in turn
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Dinah Carr died young, Noah married her sister Mary, and most of their children scattered across New York and Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: He was wounded storming Kennesaw Mountain
narrative_blocks.body: Urbane Hart served with the 11th Michigan Infantry and was wounded at Kennesaw Mountain in 1864 before mustering out the next year.

narrative_blocks.header: She died the same week as her mother
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline died at about ten years old in late February 1812. Her mother followed her to the grave within the week.

bio.bio_blurb: Seth Hart's son who settled in New York

cross_connections.display_label: made Britannia lamps as Bradley made brass ones in Meriden

narrative_blocks.header: He made his life in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Of Seth's children, Lucius left Connecticut for New York City, where he married Hannah Robinson. He made his life there until his death in 1871, far from the Rocky Hill of his childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Half the pewter firm Boardman & Hart
narrative_blocks.body: For two decades Hart was the New York partner in Boardman & Hart, one of the country's leading makers of Britannia and pewter ware. The firm's wares sold across the country from its Manhattan showrooms.

narrative_blocks.header: His pewter survives in museum collections
narrative_blocks.body: Tea sets, urns, and beakers stamped Boardman & Hart and Lucius Hart & Co. survive today in collections of early American pewter. Examples are held by museums documenting the nineteenth-century metalware trade.

narrative_blocks.header: A tailor's granddaughter who married a commodore
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah, granddaughter of a Rocky Hill tailor, married the banker and yachtsman E.C. Benedict in 1859. She presided over Indian Harbor, his estate on the Greenwich shore.

narrative_blocks.header: A lost son broke her health for good
narrative_blocks.body: When her only son Frederick was killed in a 1901 automobile accident, Sarah's health collapsed. She remained under a physician's care until her own death seven years later.

career.notes: Continued his father’s firm with Charles E. Huntington after 1871.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost all four of his children young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Hart Jr. and his wife buried all four of their children, none living to carry on the family name. The losses left him without direct heirs at his death in New York in 1898.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried on his father's metalware firm
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Hart Jr. learned the silverplating trade in his father's New York house, and on the elder Hart's death in 1871 he and Charles E. Huntington kept the business alive. They renamed it the Lucius Hart Manufacturing Company, continuing the Britannia and metalware line the firm was known for.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm's wares survive in museum collections
narrative_blocks.body: The Hart workshops turned out Britannia, pewter, and silverplated goods for the American household trade through the middle of the century. Pieces bearing the Hart marks are preserved today in collections such as the Winterthur Library.

narrative_blocks.header: He drove a wagon six weeks to Illinois
narrative_blocks.body: In 1834 Norman Hart and his wife Marcia drove from Connecticut to Adams County, Illinois, six weeks on the road, settling finally in Otho, Iowa, by 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found the Otho Congregational Church
narrative_blocks.body: With his wife and grown children, Hart was a charter member of the Otho Congregational Church when it organized in 1855.

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Otho's first schoolteacher and country doctor

narrative_blocks.header: He taught Otho's first school
narrative_blocks.body: George Dwight Hart taught the first school in Otho Township, served in the Civil War with the 32nd Iowa Infantry, and later became a country doctor.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

death.cause: Year only; month/day approximate per project convention. Source: 'died 1761 aged 63.'

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.
